body:
create a data generator that uses an extension property: _x-gen-type_

This property takes a string which is the data generator for that particular type.

for example for a person there might be the following extras:

``` yaml
definitions:
  Person:
    properties:
      firstName:
        type: string
        x-gen-type: firstname
      lastName:
        type: string
        x-gen-type: lastname
      age:
        type: number
        format: int32
        x-gen-type: range:10-50
      createdAt:
        type: string
        format: date-time
        x-gen-type: now
```

there are certain generators that will need to take arguments. The syntax above is just a proposal, welcoming suggestions.

The idea is that you can serve a swagger spec to test clients against, and it will know how to generate meaningful data.
So that when you have a backend team implementing a new application, the frontend team can already work with the stubbed out data.  The application should still look kind of acceptable. 
Of course for blurbs of text there is lorem ipsum and so on.

This can be made more useful for testing when it's combined with fuzzer.

body:
> Sometimes, we'll grab query parameters directly from the incoming request, rather than populating them in a struct.
> 
> For example, we might do something like this in our code:

``` go
tagType := req.URL.Query().Get("type")
```

> where req is a typical http.Request pointer.
> 
> I'd like to somehow add a swagger parameter notation directly to that piece of code.
> However, the only way I found to create swagger parameters for that situation is to create a struct and add the parameter into the struct, and add the appropriate go-swagger annotations.
> 
> Is there a way to add parameters without creating a struct first?

At this moment it’s not possible to add a comment to describe a parameter type. It’s not set in stone if that’s the right thing to do or not (so not opposed to changing that). However the reasoning is that I wanted to leverage the go type systems as much as possible and only use comments for things the go type system doesn’t allow to express very well. 

As a workaround you can do this (not very elegant though)

``` go
tag := struct{ /* swagger:parameter type */ Type string }{ Type: req.URL.Query().Get(“type”) } 
```

Currently the library supports generating parameters from an operation by analyzing a struct definition for its type and naming information. Perhaps it would be good to allow for defining the type and naming information as part of the comment annotation.

body:
Add support for server sent events

This includes a proposal to swagger-spec repo for an extension.

Server sent events are a specific mime type, currently in swagger 2.0 there is no support for different responses based on request criteria like content type.

In this proposal the idea is that the only possible response from that endpoint is a sse-eventstream

``` yaml
paths:
  /:
    get:
      produces: ["text/event-stream"]
      responses:
        default:
          description: example of defining events for sse 
          schema:
            type: object
            x-events:
              added:
                type: object
                properties:
                  message:
                    type: string
                  createdAt:
                    type: string
                    format: date-time
              updated:
                type: object
                  properties:
                    previous:
                      type: string
                    newValue:
                      type: string
                    updatedAt:
                      type: string
                      format: date-time
```

related: https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/issues/396

body:
We are using a swagger model in which the produced and consumed media type for all operations is "application/xml". Sometimes, the XML element names used are different from the generated type names, and all elements belong to a custom XML namespace, for example:

``` yaml
tuple:
  type: object
  properties:
...
  xml:
    namespace: http://www.sap.com/lmsl/slp
```

In all generated model structs, there should be a field of type `xml.Name` that contains the namespace specified in `xml.namespace` and the correct XML element name, for example:

``` go
type Tuple struct {
    XMLName xml.Name `xml:"http://www.sap.com/lmsl/slp tuple"`
```

As reported in #353, there is currently no support for media type "application/xml" at all. However, once it is added, naming of XML elements and support for XML namespaces should be considered as well.
title: Generated models don't have proper XML element names and namespaces when the media type is application/xml

body:
We are using a swagger model in which the produced and consumed media type for all operations is "application/xml". The model contains "wrapped" XML arrays, for example:

``` yaml
files:
  type: array
  items:
    $ref: "#/definitions/File"
  xml:
    namespace: http://www.sap.com/lmsl/slp
    wrapped: true
```

Currently, array types are always generated simply as arrays, e.g. `type Files []*File`. Instead, if `xml.wrapped` is `true`, they should be generated as structs containing arrays. There should also be a field of type `xml.Name` that contains the namespace specified in `xml.namespace` and the correct XML element name (see #354). 

``` go
type Files struct {
    XMLName xml.Name `xml:"http://www.sap.com/lmsl/slp files"`
    Files   []*File  `xml:"File,omitempty"`
}
```

Consequently in the `Validate` function, `m.Files` should be used instead of `m` to refer to the array.

Similarly, arrays within object types are also always generated simply as arrays, e.g. `Parameters []*Parameter`. Instead, if `xml.wrapped` is `true`, they should also be generated as structs containing arrays, for example:

``` go
type ProcessParameters struct {
    Parameters []*Parameter `xml:"Parameter,omitempty"`
}

type Process struct {
...
  Parameters ProcessParameters `xml:"parameters,omitempty"`
}
```

I believe in this case it's better to generate a named struct type instead of anonymous one, as the corresponding field is then much easier to initialize.
title: Generated models don't support wrapped XML arrays properly when the media type is application/xml

body:
Our legacy application supports Swagger paths like the following:

```
POST /v3/items/{name}?type=key&size={size}
```

where both _{name}_ and _{size}_ are defined as path parameters.

In Go-Swagger, we cannot include '?' and '=' characters into the path definition.

What would be the best suggestion for us, if we have to preserve this style?  

On one side, we do not wish to add query parameter _{type}_ with fixed value 'key' (we assume it will appear in the generated Java API), and on other side, we cannot change the path to `/v3/items/key/{name}`, even if we think it is a good idea.

We are considering a workaround using _%3F_ for '?' and _%3D_ for '='.

When doing `200: body:SomeResponse` in responses, `SomeResponse` won't get included in the definitions. Introduced by #596.

Here's a broken test case to demonstrate: https://github.com/bfirsh/go-swagger/commit/5ae46516fd61c0eb33365c1467eb4144c0bc5d03

Some digging through the code suggests that `postDecls` keeps track of what is added to definitions through some kind of discovery process, and when `setOpResponses.Parse()` parses the responses it should do _something_ with that.

The discovery process is very tangled and undocumented though so I can't figure out what it should do, so maybe this is more obvious to someone who knows how this bit of code works...

This is what I had to do to make the api client work:

``` go
package main

import (
    "fmt"

    "github.com/org/project/pkg/api-client/client/repositories"
    "github.com/go-openapi/strfmt"

func main() {
    runtime := httptransport.New("172.17.0.1", "/", []string{"https"})
    runtime.DefaultAuthentication = httptransport.APIKeyAuth("cookie", "header", "session=blah; csrftoken=blah")

I would like the generator to use (decimal.Decimal)[https://github.com/shopspring/decimal] type to represent fields with type: number, format: decimal. The decimal format is not a part of the OpenAPI specs (as far as I can tell) so I can't expect this to be available in go-swagger. How ever, float64 is not acceptable for handling money. 

Is there a way to plug-in / register custom format/types to the generator? I'm aware of strfmt but it doesn't seem to be possible to define the string formats for the generator (just when you're generating specs from code)

I would also like to configure the generator to use int instead of int64 for integers etc. I understand this is not necessarily ideal for everyone which is why I'd like it to be configurable. 

## Swagger specification

    Money:
      type: object
      properties:
        amount:
          type: number
          format: decimal # non standard
          description: Amount is the monetary value
        currency:
          type: string
          format: currency
          description: 3 char currency code  # https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_4217

----

Any way to do this now, any plans to support this in the future or would you accept this feature (configurable custom formats/types in the generator) if I find a solution?

body:
Currently go-swagger only supports generating go code.
The entire generation process is guided by a struct GenOpts which has LanguageOpts that encapsulate language specific information like keywords, formatting function etc.

For example to add a markdown generator I created MarkdownOpts a configuration for dealing with the markdown language. In addition to that I added a template to generate markdown based on the object model described in generator/structs.go

https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/c9e1861c3faf8438644cd753bd36b892c934afd2/generator/support.go#L35-L56
https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/c9e1861c3faf8438644cd753bd36b892c934afd2/generator/shared.go#L107-L112

The tempates and configuration are described in: https://goswagger.io/use/template_layout.html

So the entire system can be driven by a configuration file, but it still needs some work to make it a good system to generate code in many different languages. 

The main gap to bridge is the lack of a formatting function that isn't goimports. The simplest way is to have a external command that can be run on a file after generating.

The integration probably takes the form of an extra arg on the server, client, operation, model and support generators. It can then look up the language and section configuration in the config file.
title: Allow supporting more languages

body:
## Problem statement

As far as I'm aware, vendor extensions are currently not supported in the Generator. Vendor Extensions are properties that are prefixed with `x-` and their meaning is defined only by the ones using that extension.

The value of such an extension property can be `null`, a `primitive` or an `object`. Which type exactly gets chosen will depend completely on the meaning behind it.

More information can be found at: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#vendorExtensions

## Example

A use-case example could be where a user has a custom template, where some code should only be generated for some routes, and not for others. Currently this behavior is not possible, but could be achieved with this feature.

In this example one could define a certain boolean property called `x-foobar`. This could then for example be used in templates via an if check as follows:

```go
{{ if .foobar }}
// if the `x-foobar` property is present, this comment will be generated
{{ end }}
```

Currently I can only think of the use-case given above. But I'm sure other use-cases could come out of this. Ideas and feedback for this issue are more then welcome.
title: Support vendor extensions in Generator

A swagger (2.0) spec can be generated using annotations (Godoc comments). These are well documented for the most part on https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/tree/master/docs/generate/spec.

Yet, to a beginner this might not be sufficient to make it all comprehensible. Therefore it might help a lot to provide a begin-to-end tutorial about providing annotations to an existing (service) code base, and how you can go-swagger to then generate a spec and client from it. Keeping your spec, documentation and client always in sync with your existing service codebase.

Originally suggested by @nathany in issue #559

body:
Currently it is not possible to capture multiple segments via path templating.

Officially it is not a thing yet in Swagger 2.0 API / OpenAPI, and an open issue about it can be found here: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/291

However, on the condition that we don't break any existing behavior, we should perhaps try to support this in a practical way as possible, as capturing multiple segments at the end of a path, is a very common paradigm when mapping directly to a file system (local or in the cloud).

It got proposed in swagger-js, but the maintainers seem to block it: https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-js/issues/672, however I hope we can be more liberal towards this clearly missing feature.

## Example

1. define a path as `/foo/{bar}`
2. try to call your running service with the above defined path via: `/foo/bar/baz`

Result: you'll get a 404

Normally that kind of stuff gets defined with an asterix (`*`), but even that doesn't make it work here, and does not evan validate via `swagger validate` (does validate via swagger editor).

## Proposal

In issue 291, linked above, the following (trivial) proposal is made:

> Option 1
We add support for operators + . / ; in path segments. We allow both prefix and expansion modifiers but only support array parameters. This means the only part of the spec that would require updating is the Path Templating section. A standard 6570 parsing library could be used by tooling libraries.
This would allow for optional path segments and a dynamic number of path segments by using arrays and the expansion modifier. It would also allow for unescaped parameters.
The risk of allowing unescaped parameters is that it would make tools like swagger inflector difficult to implement as it becomes challenging to match URIs to templates when parameters can contain URL delimiter characters. The common scenario for unescaped parameters is when the host is being parameterized but considering the paths property doesn't include the host, I'm not sure what other use cases exist.
title: Allow multiple segments capture with path templating

body:
## Problem statement

Enum definitions generate type aliases when defined in the separate _definitions_ section, but those same type aliases are not generated when defining the enum as a property of an inline definition.

Type aliases also don't seem to be generated when defined as a parameter, inline or not.

Basically type aliases for enum values should always be generated as constants, where possible. This to prevent from having to hardcode these values otherwise when used (indirectly) within handler and/or client code.

Originally proposed in issue #861
title: Generate type aliases for enums where possible

body:
Right now the configuration file (https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/master/generator/shared.go#L242) generated for the restapi (server) is generated once, and not generated once it exists. The reason for this is because it is meant for manual editing.

Personally I don't use the file, as I like to keep my custom code separate from all the files found in my project's `/gen` folder. This makes this _configure_ file a liability, as it will make the project not compile any longer if I remove something from my API (such as a route, producer or consumer), after the initial generation. The reason for this is because it will try to assign some handler, producer- or consumer that simply no longer exists.

It would be great if this file could become optional, such that I don't get it in my projects, and thus also not have to worry that an automated toolchain might break my project when an API changes. Personally I would make the config file generation opt-in, such that you have to specify a flag if you want this file. @casualjim what is your opinion on this?
title: make configure_<name>.go generated restapi file optional

body:
## Problem statement

Models can be used as errors, when used for an non-200 status code. This is done by formatting the model value directly with `%+v`, which will simply format the models using their Go Syntax. This works pretty great, except when the properties of the model are of a pointer type, in which case the address of the property instead of its value get printed.

This makes that the resulted error messages aren't too great. Take for example a model which simply defines a `*string* message property. With the code as it is right now, it will simply print the address of that message string, rather than the actual message. Thus making the resulting error messages not as effective as they could be.

labels.description: Related to swagger generate model command

body:
## Problem statement

This regexp is valid in JavaScript and (so as a `pattern` schema constraint in Swagger) but rejected by `swagger validate`:
`^[a-z](?=.{0,20}$)(?:[a-z0-9_]{0,4}:[a-z0-9_])?[a-z0-9_]{0,20}$`

In this regexp the assertion enforces that the whole string is always at most 21 characters long.

This cause of this failure is probably that the Go `regexp` package recognizes a smaller regexp syntax subset than Perl and JavaScript: it doesn't recognizes `(?= ... )` (zero-width positive look-ahead assertion).

## Swagger specification

## Steps to reproduce

Use the regexp in a `pattern` in a schema definition for a type `string`.

## Environment
swagger version: 248eca73c69f2d5f0dbc94d49299e028c675960e
go version: 1.7.5

labels.name: validator
labels.description: Related to codegen generation of validations

_The documentation for the extensions are available [here](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-swagger-extensions.html)_
 
_Implementing this requires support for handling extensions as requested in #839_

_General information about [Lambda](https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/) and [Api Gateway](https://aws.amazon.com/api-gateway/) is available at [Amazon WebServices](https://aws.amazon.com)_ 

_AWS Lambda does not support Go natively and a shim is needed to execute the Go binary, one such shim this effort can be based on is [this one](https://github.com/eawsy/aws-lambda-go-shim)._

_Example project to show structure and artifacts (not generated) is/will be [here](https://github.com/emsa23/go-swagger-on-aws)._

### Considerations:

#### Should the model be based on the swagger spec or the mapping?

If the model is based on the swagger spec then the body mapping needs to be limited to ```$input.json('$')``` ie complete http body. If the any mapping is to be supported the mapper needs to analyze the mapping and understand the [JsonPath](http://goessner.net/articles/JsonPath/) syntax used in the mappings.
title: Add support for AWS API Gateway and Lambda vendor extension

body:
## Problem statement

When a name collision occurs, go-swagger silently picks one definition to use and drops the rest. While we can work around this by avoiding name collisions, it is difficult to track down on sufficiently large and disjoint web APIs.

## Steps to reproduce

I'm working to fully document a web API that uses Google ProtoBuf to communicate with several backend services. Each service provides its own .proto file, and the Go code is generated from the .proto file directly. Currently, the web server uses these generated types directly, but we have some name collisions between services. I am working around this issue by duplicating the types in question and providing unique names based on the service they're associated with. 

I would like to receive some sort of output/logging/warning that a name collision was detected so that I can fail my build process and have the developer correct the name collision prior to continuing. This would help head off potential API issues right when they're introduced, rather than much later when we're testing out the documentation layer.

## Environment
swagger version: 0.10.0 
go version: 1.8.3  
OS: Windows 10
title: Detect and report name collisions

## Problem statement

Hi there,

I'm trying to use an anchor in my swagger file so I can have multiple parameters be shared across all paths. I am aware that parameters can be defined in the global parameters section and be referenced multiple places, but I'd rather use an anchor so I can just reference the list of parameters that I want to place in all my paths (rather than repeating the reference to each parameter for each path).

The following works:
```
parameters:
  RequestIdParam:
    name: X-Request-ID
    in: header
    type: string

paths:
  /objects:
      parameters:
      - $ref: '#/parameters/RequestIdParam'
      get:
        summary: "Lists the objects"
        ...
```

However this does not work:
```
parameters:
  RequestIdParam:
    name: X-Request-ID
    in: header
    type: string

DefaultParameters: &DEFAULT_HEADERS
  parameters:
  - $ref: '#/parameters/RequestIdParam'

paths:
  /objects:
      <<: *DEFAULT_HEADERS
      get:
        summary: "Lists the objects"
        ...
```

I'm confused as to why the merge is not picking up the path-level parameters. Am I missing something obvious here?

body:
## Problem statement
If some content type like "image/png" or "*/*" is specified in "produces" part schema, generated client does not accept those content types and errors out with 'no consumer: "image/png"'.

Specifically, we use a schema like the one below and respond to user requests with binary data and set content-type header manually based on mime-type of the data. It works fine if you access it from curl or web browser but errors in go-swagger generated client.

## Swagger specification
```
swagger: "2.0"
info:
  title: Test
  version: 0.0.1
schemes:
- "http"
paths:
  /test:
    get:
      produces:
        - "application/octet-stream"
        - "*/*"
      responses:
        200:

I am quite new to Swagger and I'd like to know if it's possible to define path parameters for a `swagger:route` without having to define new structs for them.

My endpoint is:

```
/*
swagger:route GET /{country} getCountryDetails

Returns details for the given country

	Produces:
	  - application/json

	Responses:
	  200: Match
	  500: Error
*/
r.Path("/geofence/v1/{country}").Methods("GET").HandlerFunc(stat(getCountryResource, withProxyStatName("get-country-resource")))
```

It would be very nice if I could just describe `country` as a string in that comment, like so:

```
/*
swagger:route GET /{country} getCountryDetails
        
Parameters: country string in:path
...
*/
```

Is that possible? Or do I have to create a struct as a wrapper for a string?

Thanks!

body:
It's a bit confusing, but logical...

`alias swagger="docker run --rm -it -v $HOME:$HOME -w $(pwd) quay.io/goswagger/swagger"`

If $(pwd) is not a subdirectory of $HOME, then workdir inside launched docker container will be empty and swagger command will output error that it can't find swagger.json.

Solution:
Should be this way:
`alias swagger="docker run --rm -it -v $(pwd):$(pwd) -w $(pwd) quay.io/goswagger/swagger"`
Then swagger.json will be available.
title: Docker run command in README is not working if not in home dir/subdir

body:
## Problem statement
It seems as if imports are being resolved after each file is generated, which is pretty cool! There are timing issues that end up causing undesired side-effects, however.

The most important issue is that it becomes very important which order I run my custom templates. If I run a template that relies on code from a template that gets generated later on, then at best the import resolution will just not include any references to that package. I've seen, however, that it can also pick up undesired imports from other projects, most notably from repositories in which I've forked code and run code generation on a very similar OAS spec.

I'm not sure if there's a decent solution to this, but could the `goimport` task be run after all code is generated, instead of after each individual file? 

My current workaround is that I plan to include imports in my custom code generation templates. I was hoping to be able to be lazier than that, though :)

## Steps to reproduce
I've put together a complete example here: https://github.com/dmacthedestroyer/goswagger-import-example
## Environment
swagger version: dev (head)
go version: 1.9.2
OS:   darwin/amd64
title: Custom Templating: goimports and timing issues

body:
## Problem statement
Whenever a schema declares a default value, the corresponding generated structure is properly initialized, like in:
```yaml
parameters:
  - in: body
    name: myBodyParam
    schema:
      type: object
      properties:
        p1: 
          type: string
        p2:
           type: integer
      default:
        p1: abc
        p2: 12
```

However, when defaults are declared at a lower level, no initialization takes place, as in:
```yaml
parameters:
  - in: body
    name: myBodyParam
    schema:
      type: object
      properties:
        p1: 
          type: string
          default: abc
        p2:
          type: integer
          default: 12
```

It looks like the "schema" term used in the [json schema spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-fge-json-schema-validation-00#section-6.2) has been taken to the letter, making it equivalent to the swagger schema term.


## Steps to reproduce
generate server with structures proposed  as examples and submit uninitialized body

swagger version: x.x.x

body:
I'm trying to follow the [seemingly](https://peter.bourgon.org/blog/2017/06/09/theory-of-modern-go.html) [dominant](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yszygk1cpEc) go advice of avoiding globals and passing dependencies into the objects that require them. I'm trying to sort out what the best patterns for doing so with go-swagger handlers are. 

Right now I'm using the *HandlerFunc(handler) version of handlers that is documented in the examples, but I'm thinking that in order to give the handlers state I should be defining a struct for each handler, define Handle() on it, and set that to be the handler of the API object. Then I could add the logger and the dbconnection to those structs and be able to pass that in.

In this case, it seems a bit silly that each handler-struct will end up with the same two fields but different implementations of the Handle() function, is there a way to invert that?

At first I was thinking that the context package might be a good way to do this (I know that I want to use context to collect structured logging for each request) but upon further reading it appears that contexts are created anew on each request so it's not the right place for this state to live. 

I'd love to hear how different people are solving this problem, I'd like to avoid making package global variables if possible.
title: Good Patterns For Passing State Into Handlers?

body:
## Problem statement

I generated a client from the Open API Spec 2.0 with the latests go-swagger.

The yaml file contains  securityDefinitions:

```
security:
- apiKeyHeader: []
  oauth2CLX API: []
- apiKeyQuery: []
  oauth2CLX API: []

securityDefinitions:
  apiKeyHeader:
    type: "apiKey"
    name: "Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key"
    in: "header"
  apiKeyQuery:
    type: "apiKey"
    name: "subscription-key"
    in: "query"
  oauth2CLX API:
    type: "oauth2"
    tokenUrl: "https://backend.api.clx.com/Token"
    flow: "password"
    scopes: {}
````

When the client is generated, I don't see any options to pass the two header arguments `apiKey ` and `Bearer` 

In the docs I found the FAQ section: [Can we set a User-Agent header?](https://goswagger.io/faq/faq_client.html#can-we-set-a-user-agent-header)

Unfortunately its not possible to use the **custom transport** solution as only **one** header can be set. The spec requires however two headers.

The only feasible option is to **intercept a http requests** however I was expecting that the generated code has arguments to set `securityDefinitions` in some way so that the client can just do the calls.

labels.description: API authentication

title: Generated Client is ignoring security definitions in OpenAPI Spec

I would like to know what is the reason we map an untyped json object as interface{} rather than map[string]interface{} in models.
The generator understands this is a map but still, it generates an interface.

```
swagger: "2.0"

body:
## Problem statement

I observe that slices and maps elements are nullable only when referring to a complex element (i.e. len(properties)>0) or to an explicitly x-nullable element.

I observe too that we have some specific rules to render primitive types as pointers (based on validations and default).

When these rules apply, however, I am facing some inconsistencies between the composed type (e.g. []int64 or map[string]int64) and the element type (e.g. *int64).

Everything works well until I decide, for instance to set a "minimum: 0" validation on the element.

At this point, the element type is a pointer and the map / slice type thinks its element is not a pointer...
resulting in broken codegen.

Everything works fine again if I specify x-nullable (everyone agrees this is a pointer then...).

I could work around this in dev, resolving primitive pointers as pointers in slices and maps, but found out this breaks the PointerLifting test, which assumes this should never be the case.

Is there any particular reason why this test assumes we never want pointers as elements?

## Swagger specification
Here are two examples:
```yaml
definitions:
  aSlice:    # <- works because it defines an aliased type
    type: array
    items:
     type: integer
     minimum: 0
  aMap: # <- breaks
    type: object
    additionalProperties:
      type: integer
      minimum: 0

  aMapOfSlice: # <- breaks
    type: object
    additionalProperties:
      type: array
      items:
        type: integer
        minimum: 0
```
## Steps to reproduce

## Environment
swagger version: x.x.x  
go version: x.x.x  
OS:

body:
## Problem statement
In order to ensure that a field is included into responses (doesn't have `omitempty` struct field attribute) it should be listed in the required array. On the other hand if the same field can be `null` it should have `x-nullable: true` property set. This works fine for GET requests. However the field validation on POST/PUT requests considers the `null` value as invalid that leads to an error as follows:

```
{
    "code": 602,
    "message": "field in body is required"
}
```
 
It seems the culprit is the following code in the validateField() function that checks that a not default value is provided:
```
   if err := validate.Required("field", "body", m.Field); err != nil {
          return err
      }
```

The Swagger Specification 2.0 stipulates that the `required` property is taken directly from the JSON Schema definition and follows the same specifications. The [JSON Schema Validation](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-fge-json-schema-validation-00#section-5.4.3) document states the following:
> 5.4.3.2.  Conditions for successful validation
>
>   An object instance is valid against this keyword if its property set
>   contains all elements in this keyword's array value.

Correct me if I'm wrong but it doesn't state that properties should have certain values. Only that properties should be present in an object instance. This matches the `omitempty` attribute logic but not that validation code. 

## Swagger specification
```
  definitions:
    foo:
      type: object
      required:
        - field
        - bar
      properties:
        field:
          type: string
          x-nullable: true
        bar:
          type: object
          x-nullable: true
          required:
            - baz
          properties:
            baz:
              type: string
```


## Steps to reproduce
1. Generate server
1. Run it
1. Send POST or PUT request with an object that has the field set to `null`

## Environment
swagger version: 0.13.0  
go version: 1.10.2
OS: Linux

## Problem statement

I would like to use [Dredd](https://github.com/apiaryio/dredd) to test the generated Swagger against the API. The problem is I'm using path parameters which required a default or an example by Dredd, but since it's a required field, I have to use an example value. I don't think Swagger can generate the vendor extension I need because when I add `// x-example: USERID` to the struct, it will remove the "x-" in the spec which is not the behavior I need.

## Swagger specification

This is the spec it generates with `example` even thought I specify `x-example` in the Go code annotation.

```json
...
        "summary": "Delete a user.",
        "operationId": "UserDestroy",
        "parameters": [
          {
            "type": "string",
            "example": "USERID",
            "x-go-name": "UserID",
            "name": "user_id",
            "in": "path",
            "required": true
          }
        ],
...

This is the spec I need with `x-example`:

        "summary": "Delete a user.",
        "operationId": "UserDestroy",
        "parameters": [
          {
            "type": "string",
            "x-example": "USERID",
            "x-go-name": "UserID",
            "name": "user_id",
            "in": "path",
            "required": true
          }

...

## Problem statement
When I set the multipleOf value with a float point number, the validation may not always work.
For example, if I set the value as multipleOf 0.01, most of the time the number is totally fine, but occasionally it will returning with `XXX in body should be a multiple of xxx`

However, when it trying to eventually calling for *swag.IsFloat64AJSONInteger*, it will convert it into int or uint and compare to be sure. body:
## Problem statement

I'm trying to use swagger to define [HAL structures](http://stateless.co/hal_specification.html) and having some problems with dynamic keys (eg a key like `lf:create-module`) in the schemas, where the client library can not Unmarshal additionalProperties when nested deep in a structure, as the values in `map[string]*HalHref` are null. When json.Unmarshall occurs, and the values for `additionalProperties` are unmarshalled an error is thrown:

```json
{
  "error": "json: Unmarshal(nil *models.HalHref)"
}
```

This is being thrown in the built in json library in [https://github.com/golang/go/blob/master/src/encoding/json/decode.go#L172](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/master/src/encoding/json/decode.go#L172) where `v` is the `toadd` variable mentioned above:

```go
	rv := reflect.ValueOf(v)
	if rv.Kind() != reflect.Ptr || rv.IsNil() {
		return &InvalidUnmarshalError{reflect.TypeOf(v)}
	}
```

I can fix this by changing `var toadd *HalHref` to `toadd := &HalHref{}` in the `additionalProperties` functions (see Swagger Specification section). 

I tried overriding the template, but it's a protected template, so I can't override it. If this is a bug, I'm happy to do a PR, but I'd be looking for guidance on how to write test cases around the *functionality* of the generated code, as I can't seem to find any at the moment.

My code looks like:


## Swagger specification

```yaml
definitions:
  hal-rsc-links:
    type: object
    description: Links to this resources and documentation for this resource
    properties:
      curies:
        type: array
        x-omitempty: true
        items:
          $ref: "#/definitions/hal-curie"
    additionalProperties:
      $ref: "#/definitions/hal-href"
  hal-href:
    type: object
    properties:
      href:
        type: string
  hal-curie:
    type: object
    properties:
      name:
        type: string
      href:
        type: string
        format: uri
      templated:
        type: boolean
```
https://github.com/getlunaform/lunaform/blob/6055306666424ced718514d90c0f27ea6e59665e/swagger.yml#L779

This generates a model like:

```go
// UnmarshalJSON unmarshals this object with additional properties from JSON
func (m *HalRscLinks) UnmarshalJSON(data []byte) error {
        //
	// ... Remove code for brevity
        //

	// stage 3, add additional properties values
	if len(stage2) > 0 {
		result := make(map[string]*HalHref)
		for k, v := range stage2 {
			var toadd *HalHref
			if err := json.Unmarshal(v, toadd); err != nil {
				return err
			}
			result[k] = toadd
		}
		m.HalRscLinks = result
	}

	return nil
}
```

and the generic resource parameters passed in from the client look like:

```go
// NewCreateModuleParams creates a new CreateModuleParams object
// with the default values initialized.
func NewCreateModuleParams() *CreateModuleParams {
	var ()
	return &CreateModuleParams{

		timeout: cr.DefaultTimeout,
	}
}
```

## Steps to reproduce

If possible, this can be reproduced in my project repo.

1. `cd $GO/src/getlunaform/lunaform`
2. `make generate`
3. `dep ensure`
4. `go run cmd/lunaform-server/main.go --port 8080 --scheme=http --api-key=dev-key`
5.  Generate a config file for the client
```bash
mkdir -p ~/.config/
cat > ~/.config/lunaform.yaml <<EOF
---
host: localhost
port: 8080
schemes:
  - http
log:
  level: error
apikey: dev-key
EOF
```
6. Run the command to get the error
```bash
go run cli/main.go terraform \
    module \
    create \
    --name tf-vpc  \
    --source github.com/drewsonne/tf-vpc \
    --type git
```

## Environment
swagger version: 0.15.0 - 84da4f998602ef20ebd60a576756996162a1ca66 
go version: 1.10.3 
OS:   darwin/amd64

```shell

Each of my "plugins" have a separate schema because they're different, isolated things. However, they are not so isolated that they deserve their own repo or Go module.

Now I try to do this:

```
$ swagger generate model -f ./plugins/test1/swagger.yml -t ./plugins/test1
2018/09/01 14:47:52 validating spec /Users/alex/Trash/swag/plugins/test1/swagger.yml

2018/09/01 14:47:52 building a plan for generation

I don't understand why it's so strict. What should it matter what the parent package is?

Note that it works if I put a dummy `go.mod` inside `./plugins/test1`, but that's not what I want.

Ok, so let's try using slashed package name instead:

```shell
$ swagger generate model -f ./plugins/test1/swagger.yml -m plugins/test1
2018/09/01 14:49:00 validating spec /Users/alex/Trash/swag/plugins/test1/swagger.yml

## Problem statement

I have randomly been seeing `nil` principals for API methods generated in go-swagger where there shouldn't be and it changes randomly on compile (e.g. if I run the API Server 5 times, 2 of those times I'll have no issues, and the other 3 I'll experience `nil` principals in 3 DIFFERENT API endpoints).

My API requires both an API Key and Oauth2 security for all endpoints, where only the latter can generate a proper principal object, the former just validates the key and returns a `nil` principal. The order of the security definition in the YAML file is reflective of this need.

My guess is that the security definition is placed into a Go `map` and then iterated over which doesn't guarantee order when validating the incoming request.

@casualjim was able to confirm this in the Slack channel, some relevant comments of his ([discussion link](https://goswagger.slack.com/archives/C04R30YMU/p1536335982000100)):

> together with: https://github.com/go-openapi/spec/blob/master/swagger.go#L85
> https://github.com/go-openapi/spec/blob/master/operation.go#L35

```yaml
securityDefinitions:

   type: "apiKey"

body:
## Problem statement

This is surprisingly hard to summarize.

I'm trying to use a Swagger spec from a Django project, which uses integer primary keys and nullable foreign keys.  These get properly specified as optional in the Swagger spec as far as I can tell, but the generated models are using `int64` instead of `*int64`.

The generated code indicates that something along the way is correctly able to determine which parameters are required and which are optional.  Required parameters do have `Required: true` comments, and optional parameters are annotated with `omitempty`.  But the optional parameters are never pointers and the required parameters are always pointers.

Unless I'm missing something, this means that passing the zero value or empty string for an optional parameter will omit the parameter entirely.  Since 0 is a valid primary key in Django, being able to pass the zero value for integers and have it show up in the json is required.  Furthermore, though I don't need it right now, being able to pass empty strings as optional parameters also appears to be impossible--they'll get omitted.

I suspect that the rarity of needing to pass the zero value as an optional parameter coupled with Go's choice to not distinguish between pointers and non-pointers in many places has hidden this issue.  Nonetheless it does mean I can't use it with Django, since some models will have integer primary keys of 0, and I can't foreign key to them.  The difference between 0 and null/missing is important here.

I think the intent is to make all required parameters be the values themselves and all non-required parameters pointers.  Reading a bit of the code, this could break validation for required parameters set to the zero value, i.e. `{"id": 0}`, because it checks for the presence of the parameter by comparing to the type's zero value and failing validation if they're equal.

I can't offer a Swagger spec easily.  The one I have is proprietary and I'm not good enough at Swagger to quickly write one by hand.  As far as I can tell what I have is right.  My toolchain is Django Rest Framework, drf-yasg, then go-swagger with no human editing anywhere in the process, and my spec is validating fine according to go-swagger.

A couple of pieces from the generated code to talk to our logging subsystem, which I'm trying to interface with, that I have approval to share follow.  This is a model with a required message field and a number of nullable foreign keys to things the message might be associated with:

An example of a required parameter in the generated code:

```
	// Message
	// Required: true
	// Min Length: 1
	Message *string `json:"message"`
```

And one of the nullable foreign keys:

```
	// Target
	Target int64 `json:"target,omitempty"`
```

Note how if I specify 0 for target, `encoding/json` is going to omit it, but it's not a pointer so I can't specify nil either.

Am I doing something wrong?  I'm not discounting the possibility that I've missed something obvious.  This is a blocker for my current project as we don't want to maintain a bunch of very large Go clients by hand, and I'd be willing to look into fixing it once someone confirms that I've actually found a bug.  I think that any potential fix here is likely to break code, since it involves making things pointers.

## Environment
swagger version: 0.16.0 (dd867fd63c30269ac217004c102f47a1774d4f5a) download from GitHub Releases
go version: 1.10.3
OS:   linux/amd64
title: optional parameters aren't pointers but required parameters are

body:
## Problem statement
To provide some context, Our system is based on a micro-service architecture. And services communicate through HTTP-based SDK. All SDKs have some request and response definitions. We use go-swagger to generate docs for all of our services.

We found an issue caused by name conflicts between service and its dependencies. For example, serviceA has a dependency on another service, serviceB. ServiceB defined a request struct `ResultDownloadRequest` in its SDK. ServiceA uses this request struct to send a request to serviceB. However, serviceA also defined a request struct named `ResultDownloadRequest`. The behavior we observed is that every time we run`swagger generate spec -m -b ./src -o ./docs/swagger.json`, it would generate different results. It would pick up a definition from either serviceA or serviceB randomly. 

We can certainly change the request name to walk around the issue, but it also feels bad that it would pick a random one without throwing an error, which would end up with inconsistent API doc without noticing. And it's not very easy to avoid name conflicts across all of our services. Not to mention that we have to do a manual check on if we have name conflicts within not only the current service but also all of its dependencies.

I'm wondering if there's a better way to avoid this kind of name conflicts. Or if we can set any kind of flag to indicate the priority maybe?

## Environment
swagger version: 0.16.0
go version: go1.10.3 darwin/amd64
OS:  OSX

labels.name: generate spec
labels.description: Related to spec generation from code

body:
## Problem statement

If one uses a construct like 
```
type: string
format: uri
```
is a schema definition, the generated operation is missing `strfmt `import, hence not compilable

## Swagger specification
```
paths:
  /users:
    post:
      tags:
      - User
      summary: "an operation to register a new user"
      description: >-
        Registers a new user
      operationId: "registerUser"
      consumes:
      - "application/json"
      produces:
      - "application/json"
      parameters:
      - name: payload
        in: body
        description: >-
          Data for the user to be registered
        schema:
          $ref: "./Data.yaml"
        required: true
      responses:
        201:
          description: Created
          headers:
            Location:
              description: URI of the registered resource
              type: string
              format: uri
            ETag:
              description: >-
                Fingerprint of the created resource
              type: string
          schema:
            $ref: "./User.yaml"
```

Generated operation:
```
package user

// This file was generated by the swagger tool.
// Editing this file might prove futile when you re-run the swagger generate command

import (
	"net/http"

	"github.com/go-openapi/runtime"

	dto "github.xxx/bb-go/userservice/pkg/api/rest/gen/dto"
)

// RegisterUserCreatedCode is the HTTP code returned for type RegisterUserCreated
const RegisterUserCreatedCode int = 201

/*RegisterUserCreated Created

swagger:response registerUserCreated
*/
type RegisterUserCreated struct {
	/*Fingerprint of the created resource

	 */
	ETag string `json:"ETag"`
	/*URI of the registered resource

	 */
	Location strfmt.URI `json:"Location"`

	/*
	  In: Body
	*/
	Payload *dto.User `json:"body,omitempty"`
}

```
As you see for the `Location` header the generated code uses `strfmt.URI`, while `strfmt` is not imported

## Steps to reproduce

Just generate...

## Environment
swagger version: 0.17.0 
go version: 1.11  
OS:  Windows 10 (Linux subsystem/Ubuntu 18.04)

body:
## Problem statement

I'm trying to use a service with a swagger spec that looks to me like it was hand-created from an existing hand-written service (I find numerous discrepancies), and I'm trying to report issues in the spec for people to fix. Some are obvious; however, some response formatting issues (where the response almost, but not quite, matches the spec) seem to result in the following error, with different response types depending on the call: `&[] (*[]*models.ServiceConfigVersionResponse) is not supported by the TextConsumer, can be resolved by supporting TextUnmarshaler interface`.  The response from the server is 200 and includes the correct-ish response JSON. Either the JSON is not quite correct, or it's a generated code bug; for some classes I cannot see what the issue might be in json, esp. as it tends to be large.

Is it possible to improve the message to include what's actually wrong with the response? The spec is giant so having to implement unmarshalling for half the calls partially defeats the purpose of using the spec.

## Swagger specification

Attached.
[swagger.txt](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/files/2564107/swagger.txt)

## Steps to reproduce

Basically requires an Apache Ambari deployment. I generated the Go client using swagger and I'm calling the clusters/{clusterName}/configurations/service_config_versions API. I cannot paste the response fully but it follows this pattern:
`{
  "href" : "...",
  "items" : [
    {
      "href" : "...",
      "cluster_name" : "",
      "configurations" : [
        {
          "Config" : {
            "cluster_name" : "...",
            "stack_id" : "..."
          },
          "type" : "...",
          "tag" : "....",
          "version" : 1,
          "properties" : {
            "...." : "....",
          },
          "properties_attributes" : { }
        },` etc. 

## Environment
swagger version: 0.17.0
go version: 1.10.3  
OS: Mac on client, CentOS on server.
title: a better error for  &[] (*[]*models.ServiceConfigVersionResponse) is not supported by the TextConsumer, can be resolved by supporting TextUnmarshaler interface

body:
As a newcomer to both go and swagger it is not obvious how to choose between the [swaggo](https://github.com/swaggo) and [goswagger](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger) projects.
Both use different syntax for marking up REST APIs and both are capable of generating documentation.
It would be helpful if there was a page explaining the history or difference in goal of each project.

It is also unclear which syntax makes more sense.

swagger:route looks more go like
but the @ notation sems more in line with descriptions on the swagger web-site.

I think one major difference is that goswagger can also generate code from swagger definitions.
However, if you only want the documentation generation side (as I do) it is less obvious which to choose.
title: What are the differences between swaggo and go-swagger?

## Problem statement

I am using `go-swagger` to create a client to consume a swagger 2.0 API, specifically the one described by [this schema](https://products.solace.com/download/PUBSUB_SEMPV2_SCHEMA_YAML).

I am running into an issue using the generated client to update (`PATCH`) resources described by that spec: in brief, I'm unable to update attributes on resources *to* values considered "empty" by the `json` module. 

The reason is that the attributes have the  `omitempty` label is set in the structs used to define the parameters for the resources. 
*However*, per the API spec, any attributes that are not set in the REST request are not updated.

This means that it's impossible to set attributes to their default / "empty" value. What is the correct way to deal with this? 
Should I be decorating all of the attributes in the swagger spec with `x-nullable`?

## Swagger specification
The full spec is at https://products.solace.com/download/PUBSUB_SEMPV2_SCHEMA_YAML but 


## Steps to reproduce
1. Generate client from above spec
1. Create an update request for an object that sets an attribute to a default value. E.g. setting a boolean to `false`
1. Observe that the update request generated for this does not include the attribute. 

## Environment
swagger version: 2.0  
go version: `go1.10.5 linux/amd64`
OS:   Linux 64 bit: `4.17.14-202.fc28.x86_64`

body:
## Problem statement

go-swagger generates enum constants using the value of the enum as part of the variable name. This approach doesn't work if the enum constant is non-alphanumeric. In such cases, multiple constants may be generated with the same name.

## Swagger specification

A simplified (and thus, contrived) example. Note: this is based on a real spec we are trying to use:

```yaml
swagger: "2.0"
info:
  version: "1"
  title: "example"
paths:
  /example:
    get:
      parameters:
        - in: body
          name: filter
          required: true
          schema: 
            $ref: "#/definitions/Filter"
      responses:
        '200':
          description: OK


definitions:
  Filter:
    type: object
    properties:
      filter:
        type: string
        example: lt
        description: The filter to apply on the specified field
        default: =
        enum:
          - lt
          - le
          - eq
          - gt
          - ge
          - not
          - =
          - <
          - >
          - \!
```

## Steps to reproduce

Generate a client for the above spec (using latest release) and try to compile. The error received is:

```
models/filter.go:77:2: FilterFilter redeclared in this block
	previous declaration at models/filter.go:74:24
models/filter.go:80:2: FilterFilter redeclared in this block
	previous declaration at models/filter.go:77:24
```

This is caused because go-swagger cannot generate sensible names for these constants. Here's the offending part of `filter.go`:

```go
const (

	// FilterFilterLt captures enum value "lt"
	FilterFilterLt string = "lt"

	// FilterFilterLe captures enum value "le"
	FilterFilterLe string = "le"

	// FilterFilterEq captures enum value "eq"
	FilterFilterEq string = "eq"

	// FilterFilterGt captures enum value "gt"
	FilterFilterGt string = "gt"

	// FilterFilterGe captures enum value "ge"
	FilterFilterGe string = "ge"

	// FilterFilterNot captures enum value "not"
	FilterFilterNot string = "not"

	// FilterFilter captures enum value "="
	FilterFilter string = "="

	// FilterFilter captures enum value "<"
	FilterFilter string = "<"

	// FilterFilter captures enum value ""
	FilterFilter string = ""

	// FilterFilterBang captures enum value "\\!"
	FilterFilterBang string = "\\!"
)
```

## Environment
swagger version: 0.17.2 
go version: go1.11.2 linux/amd64
OS:   Ubuntu 18.04

labels.name: model
labels.description: Related to swagger generate model command

title: Enums with non-alphanumeric values generate code that doesn't compile

info:

body:
When we have nested properties with additonal properties in one of them, the json serializes the innerbody first. Hence the main properties of the outer body gets added as additonal properties.

Example:
```yaml
mainBody:
    type: object
    required: ['name', 'type']
    properties:
      name: 
        type: string
      type:
        type: string
    additionalProperties: true

mainReadonly:
      type: object
      properties:
         createdTime:
               type: string


  wholeBody:
    allOf:
      - $ref: '#/definitions/mainBody'
      - $ref: '#/definitions/mainReadonly'
```
In this case, the assetReadonly properties also get added to the additonal properties since the swagger does the serializtion of assetbody first and sees it has additonal properties. So now my serialized params look like
type,
name
type
createdTime
additionalProperties: createdTime

labels.name: model
labels.description: Related to swagger generate model command

title: Main properties added as additional Properties

I have generated a client which uploads and downloads the file by making a POST request at the given endpoint for the api. The api requires not to have accept headers and gives me Error 406 if I mention anything in Consumes or Produces media type. body:
## Problem statement

The generated server code does not enforce properties to be `required` when they are inherited from a parent via `allOf` but set to `required` in the child. However, Swagger UI correctly shows the property to be required.

## Swagger specification
bug.yaml:
```yaml
---
swagger: "2.0"
info:
  version: 0.1.0
  title: foo

paths:
  /mypath:
    post:
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/parameters/my_param"
      responses:
        202:
          description: foo

parameters:
  my_param:
    name: my_param
    in: body
    required: true
    schema:
      $ref: "#/definitions/my_composite_body"

definitions:
  body_base:
    type: object
    properties:
      optional_property:
        type: string
      required_property:
        type: string
    required:
      - required_property
  my_composite_body:
    allOf:
      - $ref: "#/definitions/body_base"
      - type: object
        required:
          - optional_property
```

## Steps to reproduce

```console
swagger generate server -f bug.yaml
```

In `models/my_composite_body.go` can be seen that there is no validation performed to ensure that `optional_property` is no longer optional but now required:

```go
// MyCompositeBody my composite body
// swagger:model my_composite_body
type MyCompositeBody struct {
	BodyBase

	MyCompositeBodyAllOf1
}

// ...

// Validate validates this my composite body
func (m *MyCompositeBody) Validate(formats strfmt.Registry) error {
	var res []error

	// validation for a type composition with BodyBase
	if err := m.BodyBase.Validate(formats); err != nil {
		res = append(res, err)
	}
	// validation for a type composition with MyCompositeBodyAllOf1

	if len(res) > 0 {
		return errors.CompositeValidationError(res...)
	}
	return nil
}

// ...

// MyCompositeBodyAllOf1 my composite body all of1
// swagger:model MyCompositeBodyAllOf1
type MyCompositeBodyAllOf1 interface{}
```

## Environment
swagger version: v0.18.0
go version: 1.11.5
OS: macOS 10.13.6

body:
Our use-case is that we have `common.yml` with common type definitions, and we have `service1.yml` and `service2.yml` with refs to `common.yml`, i.e:

```
...
schema:
  $ref: '../common.yml#/definitions/Status'
```

If we do `swagger generate model -f service1.yml` and `swagger generate model -f service2.yml` the definitions in `common.yml` are duplicated in both services (we generate the models for both services to different folders).
We would like a way to generate models for `common.yml` separately and then specify it when generating our services (similarly to how we use `existing-models` when generating the server and the client), for example:
`swagger generate model -f service1.yml --existing-models=../common`

The documentation for `generate models` [seems to imply that such a flag actually exists](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/master/docs/generate/model.md), btw, but it seems to be [ignored in code](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/deefce1ed5785664f09b259c444c4142020925e1/cmd/swagger/commands/generate/model.go#L39)?


An alternative solution (less preferable but maybe workable), is to allow generating model files while keeping the hierarchy of the original spec files, i.e if my folder hierarchy looks like this:

```
| common.yml
| service1
| - service1.yml
| service2
| - service2.yml
```

Then generating the models for service 1 will give me:
```
| status.go
| service1
| - service1_status.go
```

Is one of this options already possible (I didn't see any way)?

Thanks.

body:
## Problem statement

Sub-types are not emitted for a discriminated type when using go annotations to generate an OpenAPI spec.

## Annotated go

### `openapi.go`

```go
// From https://goswagger.io/use/spec/discriminated.html

// TeslaCar is a tesla car
//
// swagger:model
type TeslaCar interface {
	// The model of tesla car
	//
	// discriminator: true
	// swagger:name model
	Model() string

	// AutoPilot returns true when it supports autopilot
	// swagger:name autoPilot
	AutoPilot() bool
}

// The ModelS version of the tesla car
//
// swagger:model modelS
type ModelS struct {
	// swagger:allOf com.tesla.models.ModelS
	TeslaCar
	// The edition of this Model S
	Edition string `json:"edition"`
}

// The ModelX version of the tesla car
//
// swagger:model modelX
type ModelX struct {
	// swagger:allOf com.tesla.models.ModelX
	TeslaCar
	// The number of doors on this Model X
	Doors int32 `json:"doors"`
}

// Add a reference to tie TeslaCar into the type definitions hierarchy...

// swagger:response carResponse
type CarResponse struct {
	// in: body
	Body struct {
		Result  int
		Vehicle TeslaCar
	}
}
```

### `routes.go`

```go
...
	// swagger:route GET /car GetCar
	//
	// Gets a Tesla car
	//
	//   Produces:
	//   - application/json
	//
	//   Responses:
	//     200: carResponse
	router.Handle("/", makeCarHandler()).Methods("GET")
...
```

## Generated Swagger Specification

```yaml
basePath: /
consumes:
- application/json
definitions:
  TeslaCar:
    description: TeslaCar is a tesla car
    discriminator: model
    properties:
      autoPilot:
        description: AutoPilot returns true when it supports autopilot
        type: boolean
        x-go-name: AutoPilot
      model:
        description: The model of tesla car
        type: string
        x-go-name: Model
    type: object
    x-go-package: github.com/foo/bar
info:
  description: Package main Car API
  version: "1.0"
paths:
  /car:
    get:
      description: Gets a job document
      operationId: GetRoot
      produces:
      - application/json
      responses:
        "200":
          $ref: '#/responses/carResponse'
produces:
- application/json
responses:
  carResponse:
    schema:
      properties:
        Result:
          format: int64
          type: integer
        Vehicle:
          $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
      type: object
schemes:
- http
- https
swagger: "2.0"
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create new go project with the above code.
1. Run `swagger generate spec -o swagger.yaml`

## Note

Running the generate command with `-m/--scan-models` does force the subtypes to be emitted in the `definitions` array. However, this comes with the unfortunate side-effect of also generating a model for every `swagger:response` in `definitions` as well (see example below).

I say unfortunate since having to hand edit/cleanup the resulting spec breaks our CI/CD automation.

```yaml
definitions:
  CarResponse:
    properties:
      Body:
        description: 'in: body'
        properties:
          Result:
            format: int64
            type: integer
          Vehicle:
            $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
        type: object
    type: object
    x-go-package: github.com/foo/bar
  TeslaCar:
    description: TeslaCar is a tesla car
    discriminator: model
    properties:
      autoPilot:
        description: AutoPilot returns true when it supports autopilot
        type: boolean
        x-go-name: AutoPilot
      model:
        description: The model of tesla car
        type: string
        x-go-name: Model
    type: object
    x-go-package: github.com/foo/bar
  modelS:
    allOf:
    - $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
    - properties:
        edition:
          description: The edition of this Model S
          type: string
          x-go-name: Edition
      type: object
    description: The ModelS version of the tesla car
    x-class: com.tesla.models.ModelS
    x-go-name: ModelS
    x-go-package: github.com/foo/bar
  modelX:
    allOf:
    - $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
    - properties:
        doors:
          description: The number of doors on this Model X
          format: int32
          type: integer
          x-go-name: Doors
      type: object
    description: The ModelX version of the tesla car
    x-class: com.tesla.models.ModelX
    x-go-name: ModelX
    x-go-package: github.com/foo/bar
info:
  description: Package main Car API
  version: "1.0"
paths: {}
produces:
- application/json
responses:
  carResponse:
    schema:
      properties:
        Result:
          format: int64
          type: integer
        Vehicle:
          $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
      type: object
schemes:
- http
- https
swagger: "2.0"
```

## Environment
swagger version: `dev`
go version: `go1.12 darwin/amd64` 
OS:   macOS 10.14.3

body:
## Problem statement
Currently if the response header for content-type is not inside  runtime.ClientOperation.ConsumesMediaTypes we get a really nasty error like 
```go
&{  0 } (*models.User) is not supported by the TextConsumer, can be resolved by supporting TextUnmarshaler interface
```

As a new user of Go-Swagger this was quite intimidating and and hard to understand 

Example of my runtime.ClientOperation

```go
result, err := a.transport.Submit(&runtime.ClientOperation{
		ID:                 "User-Create",
		Method:             "POST",
		PathPattern:        "/api/v1/{customerID}/users",
		ProducesMediaTypes: []string{""},
		ConsumesMediaTypes: []string{"application/json"},
		Schemes:            []string{"http"},
		Params:             params,
		Reader:             &UserCreateReader{formats: a.formats},
		Context:            params.Context,
		Client:             params.HTTPClient,
	})
```

Since my API was not setting the content type header the default plain/text was sent, it was quite troublesome to understand what was actually going wrong here.

I propose adding the following block of code to  to the submit function (go-openapi/runtime/client/runtime.go:310)
```go
	var found bool
	for _, cmt := range operation.ConsumesMediaTypes {
		if cmt == mt {
			found = true
			break
		}
	}
	if !found {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("no consumer: response content-type %s is not inside our operation.ConsumesMediaTypes", mt)
	}
```

This would give a much cleaner error message like so
```go
no consumer: response content-type text/plain is not inside our operation.ConsumesMediaTypes
```

Happy to make a pull request if this is something that you think would be accepted
title: Better Error Reporting of Invalid Content Type

```
type ProductV1 struct {
    ProductV1AllOf0
}

type ProductV1AllOf0 struct {
    id *string
}
```

It adds an additional layer `ProductV1AllOf0` into the `ProductV1` struct. How can I get rid of the additional layer? And I only want all the fields in `product` been injected into `productV1`. How can I achieve this?
title: How to get rid of number suffix while using `allOf`?

body:
## Problem statement
I am using go-swagger to generate go data models from yaml. If your yaml has an enum tag in it, generally go-swagger will generate a set of constants in the generated models which point to each of the strings defined in the enum. However, if the enum tag is nested in an array of strings, no constants are generated.

## Steps to reproduce
### Working Example
```yaml
MyObject:
  properties:
    color:
      type: "string"
      enum: ['red', 'green', 'blue']
```

If I create a data model like the one above, go-swagger will generate a set of constants which point to the strings: "red", "green", and "blue".

### Not Working Example
```yaml
MyObject:
  properties:
    colors:
      type: "array"
      items:
        type: "string"
        enum: ['red', 'green', 'blue']
```

However, if I create a data model like this one, go-swagger will not generate a set of constants which point to the strings: "red", "green", and "blue".
## Environment
swagger version: 0.18.0  
go version: 1.12  
OS: Ubuntu 14.04

## Problem statement
I encountered this while I was trying to consume Swagger spec generated through go-swagger into AWS API Gateway.
AWS API Gateway adds some extensions, one of them being _x-amazon-apigateway-integration_
Here's an example:
```
x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
  httpMethod: GET
  passthroughBehavior: when_no_match
  responses:
    default:
      statusCode: "200"
  type: http_proxy
  uri: https://proxy-url.com
```

Now as `responses` is a recognised tag by itself, go-swagger seems to skip it and does not render the block in the resultant Swagger spec.
This issue is to add support to be able to skip such tags which have been embedded within some parent tag.

OS: macOs

api.BeforeDoRequest(func(request \*http.Request) { request.Header.Add("Authorization", fmt.Sprintf("Bearer %s", "my-special-token")) })

and in the client code, I would have something like this:

if c.beforeDoRequestFunc != nil {

## Swagger specification
```
swagger: "2.0"
info:
  description: Test
  version: "1.0.0"
  title: test
basePath: "/"

responses:
  Created:
     description: Resource was created
     headers:
         Location:
           description: Location of newly created resource
           type: string
           format: uri

paths:
  /foo:
    post:
      responses:
        201:
          $ref: '#/responses/Created'


```
## Steps to reproduce

The following docker build produces a binary with the issue : 
```
FROM docker.io/centos:latest

RUN curl -o /tmp/epel.rpm http://dl.fedoraproject.org/pub/epel/epel-release-latest-7.noarch.rpm && \
    yum install -y /tmp/epel.rpm && \
    yum install -y golang-bin && \
    mkdir -p /var/tmp/src /opt/go && \
    cd /var/tmp/src && \
    git clone https://github.com/golang/go.git && \
    cd go/src && \
    git checkout go1.12.6 && \
    GOROOT_FINAL=/opt/go ./make.bash && \
    cd .. && \
    cp -apv bin doc favicon.ico lib pkg robots.txt src misc test VERSION /opt/go/ && \
    rm -rf /var/tmp/src/go /tmp/epel.rpm && \
    yum autoremove -y golang-bin && \
    yum clean all  &&  \
    cd /usr/bin && \
    ln -s /opt/go/bin/go 


RUN yum install -y git && \
    cd /var/tmp/src && \
    git clone https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger.git && \
    cd go-swagger/cmd/swagger && \
    /opt/go/bin/go build && \
    cp -f swagger /usr/bin && \
    rm -fr /var/tmp/src && \
    yum autoremove -y git && \
    yum clean all

WORKDIR /src
ENV GOPATH /src
ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/bin/swagger"]

```

After using :

`  swagger generate server`

...

```
$ grep -C 5 ^import  restapi/operations/post_foo_responses.go
package operations

// This file was generated by the swagger tool.

import (
	"net/http"

	"github.com/go-openapi/runtime"
)

```

But the strfmt package should be included :

```
$ grep strfmt.URI restapi/operations/post_foo_responses.go
	Location strfmt.URI `json:"Location"`
func (o *PostFooCreated) WithLocation(location strfmt.URI) *PostFooCreated {
func (o *PostFooCreated) SetLocation(location strfmt.URI) {

```



## Environment
swagger version: 0.19.0 and HEAD as of 7/27
go version: 1.12.6 but have tried multiple
OS:   Fedora Core/30, RHEL 7, CentOS 7

body:
For example, structure definition:

```
type Outer struct {
	OuterField string `json:"outer_field"`
	Inner      `json:"inner"`
}

type Inner struct {
	InnerField string `json:"inner_field"`
}
```

Let's marshal 'Outer' structure to json:

```
obj := Outer{OuterField: "1234", Inner: Inner{InnerField: "5678"}}
str, _ := json.Marshal(obj)

```

Output:
> {"outer_field":"1234","inner":{"inner_field":"5678"}}

If we run Swagger for that structure we get next json object in swag docs:

I followed Authentication section in Tutorial, but generated code conflicts described example.

I tried `swagger generate server -A Todo -f ./swagger/swagger.yml` with `swagger.yml` below. It produces that code:

```go
// in configure_todo.go

        // other codes...
	api.JSONConsumer = runtime.JSONConsumer()

	api.JSONProducer = runtime.JSONProducer()
        // other codes...
}
```

```go
// in todo_api.go
type TodoAPI struct {
        // other properties...
	// BasicAuthenticator generates a runtime.Authenticator from the supplied basic auth function.
	// It has a default implementation in the security package, however you can replace it for your particular usage.
	BasicAuthenticator func(security.UserPassAuthentication) runtime.Authenticator
	// APIKeyAuthenticator generates a runtime.Authenticator from the supplied token auth function.
	// It has a default implementation in the security package, however you can replace it for your particular usage.
	APIKeyAuthenticator func(string, string, security.TokenAuthentication) runtime.Authenticator
	// BearerAuthenticator generates a runtime.Authenticator from the supplied bearer token auth function.
	// It has a default implementation in the security package, however you can replace it for your particular usage.
	BearerAuthenticator func(string, security.ScopedTokenAuthentication) runtime.Authenticator
        // other properties...
}
```

So, `api` in `func configureAPI` has `BasicAuthenticator`, `APIKeyAuthenticator`, `BearerAuthenticator`.

But, the document might expects `TodoApi`-ish struct has `KeyAuth` property.
How can I deal with this difference?

```

info:

        type: string

      completed:
        type: boolean
  error:

I have got a weird generated client, my non required fields are set as value, and required fields are as pointers.

```
	// address street
	AddressStreet string `json:"addressStreet,omitempty"`

body:
Following issue #1668 and comment from @casualjim (https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/issues/1668#issuecomment-436085049), API struct (`restapi/operations/configure_xxx.go`) uses authenticators without the context. Would it make sense to do something like this?

```diff
diff --git a/generator/templates/server/builder.gotmpl b/generator/templates/server/builder.gotmpl
index c32c9a6c..6967271a 100644
--- a/generator/templates/server/builder.gotmpl
+++ b/generator/templates/server/builder.gotmpl
@@ -43,8 +43,11 @@ func New{{ pascalize .Name }}API(spec *loads.Document) *{{ pascalize .Name }}API
     spec:                   spec,
     ServeError:             errors.ServeError,
     BasicAuthenticator:     security.BasicAuth,
+    BasicAuthenticatorCtx:  security.BasicAuthCtx,
     APIKeyAuthenticator:    security.APIKeyAuth,
+    APIKeyAuthenticatorCtx: security.APIKeyAuthCtx,
     BearerAuthenticator:    security.BearerAuth,
+    BearerAuthenticatorCtx: security.BearerAuthCtx,
     {{ range .Consumes }}{{ if .Implementation }}{{ pascalize .Name }}Consumer: {{ .Implementation }},{{else}}{{ pascalize .Name }}Consumer: runtime.ConsumerFunc(func(r io.Reader, target interface{}) error {
       return errors.NotImplemented("{{.Name}} consumer has not yet been implemented")
     }),{{end}}
```

This way both with and without context authenticators are exposed and developers can decide which one to use. There are also no backward incompatible changes done.

Just wanted to verify whether this approach is acceptable before opening the PR.

body:
## Executive summary

We use swagger to generate model and operation API for Docker Engine (aka [Moby](https://github.com/moby/moby)), and we believe that types and structures should be separated from the code. This is why we are using an option to disable generating `Validate()` methods.

Unfortunately, this option has disappeared, making us stick to a very old go-swagger codebase, which is becoming more and more problematic, e.g.
* problems with unstable/unsorted output (https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/issues/846, example: https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/38832#issuecomment-472106785)
* golang 1.13 compatibility issue (https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/issues/2058)

## Details

Both `swagger generate model` and `swagger generate operation` used to have an option to disable generating `Validate()` methods. The option name was `--skip-validator` (what was in line with other options such as `--skip-struct` or `--skip-responses`), it was added:

* for `generate model`: by commit 38dbdd808fb157f84724e19269d310128f7df71d ("move generator2 to generator package", Feb 16 2015, possibly earlier but there's no git history AFAICS)
* for `generate operation`: by commit 041580674330b64a393d7353ca13ffe4ab540924 ("Add a --skip-validator flag for generate operation", Oct 5 2016)

Unfortunately, this option was silently removed from both commands by commit d2aae8150c526d861828ee0e8a16623df01d3fb5 ("Added an option in genOpts", Jan 4, 2018).

A similarly named (but with very different semantics) option, `--skip-validation`, was added by commit 78f848e7f01c8dca5c2744c69b08d9732d60b537 ("validate spec prior to gen. client/server", Jan 8, 2017). As suggested in https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/commit/d2aae8150c526d861828ee0e8a16623df01d3fb5#commitcomment-29777292, a possible user confusion between `--skip-validation` and `--skip-validator` was the reason for removal, although it's not very clear.

We can obviously work around that by forking go-swagger and applying fixes on top of the old code, but I believe there's a better way.

## Proposed solution

Reinstate the removed functionality.

To the best of my understanding, the biggest problem here is to avoid user confusion between `--skip-validation` and `--skip-validator`. There are many ways to solve it; I propose to:
 * add `--generate` option, accepting a `string:bool` type, for example: `--generate struct:false --generate validator:false`.
* for backward compatibility, add the following hidden options
`--skip-struct` (equivalent of `--generate struct:false`)
`--skip-handler`
`--skip-parameters`
`--skip-responses`
`--skip-url-builder`
title: Option to skip Validate() methods generation is gone
