body:
Is it possible to specify an output format (json or yaml) for the generated CLI?

```diff
Usage:
  laptain [command]

Available Commands:
  [...]
  completion    Generate completion script
  help          Help about any command

Flags:
      --base-path string 
      --config string      config file path
      --debug              output debug logs
      --dry-run            do not send the request to server
+     --output -o          output format, supported are "json" or "yaml" (default "json")
  -h, --help               help for laptain
      --hostname string    hostname of the service
      --password string    password for basic auth
      --scheme string      Choose from: [https] (default "https")
      --username string    username for basic auth

Use "laptain [command] --help" for more information about a command.

```

Currently when specifying `application/x-yaml` in producers specs, its generates:
```go
// makeClient constructs a client object
func makeClient(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) (*client.LoadBalancerAPI, error) {
	hostname := viper.GetString("hostname")
	viper.SetDefault("base_path", client.DefaultBasePath)
	basePath := viper.GetString("base_path")
	scheme := viper.GetString("scheme")

	r := httptransport.New(hostname, basePath, []string{scheme})
	r.SetDebug(debug)
	// set custom producer and consumer to use the default ones

	r.Consumers["application/json"] = runtime.JSONConsumer()

	r.Producers["application/json"] = runtime.JSONProducer()

	// warning: produces application/x-yaml is not supported by go-swagger cli yet
```
title: Is is possible to ouput CLI commands output as YAML ?

body:
## Problem statement
I'm generating the spec from code annotations.

I have an error model that I want to reuse for my endpoints. One of the model's fields is a reference to the targeted endpoint. Ideally, I'd like to to have each endpoint's UI example responses use their respective endpoints in their example. As far as I'm aware, that isn't possible unless defining multiple response structs. 

Is this understanding correct?

## Swagger specification
Actual:
```yaml
paths:
  /users:
    post:
      responses:
        400:
          description: badRequestError
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  description: 
                    type: string 
                    example: request body must not be empty. 
                  error: 
                    type: string 
                    example: bad_request 
                  status: 
                    type: integer 
                    format: int64 
                    example: 400 
                  endpoint: 
                    type: string 
                    example: /api/v1/users
  /products:
    post:
      400:
        responses:
          description: badRequestError
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  description: 
                    type: string 
                    example: request body must not be empty. 
                  error: 
                    type: string 
                    example: bad_request 
                  status: 
                    type: integer 
                    format: int64 
                    example: 400 
                  endpoint: 
                    type: string 
                    example: /api/v1/users          # ideally this would read api/v1/products, if possible
```

Desired (the only difference is the last line):
```yaml
paths:
  /users:
    post:
      responses:
        400:
          description: badRequestError
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  description: 
                    type: string 
                    example: request body must not be empty. 
                  error: 
                    type: string 
                    example: bad_request 
                  status: 
                    type: integer 
                    format: int64 
                    example: 400 
                  endpoint: 
                    type: string 
                    example: /api/v1/users
  /products:
    post:
      400:
        responses:
          description: badRequestError
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  description: 
                    type: string 
                    example: request body must not be empty. 
                  error: 
                    type: string 
                    example: bad_request 
                  status: 
                    type: integer 
                    format: int64 
                    example: 400 
                  endpoint: 
                    type: string 
                    example: /api/v1/products
```

## Steps to reproduce
```go
// BadRequestError
//
// swagger:response badRequestError
type _ struct {
  // in: body
  Body struct {
    // Example: bad_request
    Error string `json:"error"`

    // Example: 400
    Code int `json:"status"`

    // Example: request body must not be empty
    Description string `json:"description"`

    // Example: /api/v1/users
    Endpoint string `json:"endpoint"`
  }
}

// swagger:route POST /users createUser
//
// Create a new user.
//
//  Responses:
//    400: badRequestError
func CreateUser(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { ... }

// swagger:route POST /products createProducts
//
// Create a new product.
//
//  Responses:
//    400: badRequestError
func CreateProduct(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { ... }
```


## Environment
swagger version: dev
go version: 1.19.3 
OS:   linux amd64

example:

I'm getting error messages like `Error: json: cannot unmarshal object into Go value of type string` for many CLI commands that return a list in a JSON format. For example: ``` keith@Keiths-MBP startree-platform % startree table listTables --debug 2023/03/19 16:09:28 Using config file: /Users/keith/.config/startree/config.yaml 2023/03/19 16:09:28 Server url: https://xxxxxxxxxxxxxxx GET /tables HTTP/1.1 Host: xxxxxxxxxxxxxxx User-Agent: Go-http-client/1.1 Accept: application/json Authorization: Basic xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx= Accept-Encoding: gzip HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Length: 145 Access-Control-Allow-Headers: * Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, OPTIONS, DELETE Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * Content-Type: application/json Pinot-Controller-Host: pinot-pinot-controller-0.pinot-pinot-controller-headless.managed.svc.cluster.local Pinot-Controller-Version: Unknown {"tables":["NYCTaxiData","NYCTaxiDataDim","promo_codes","rides","rides_test","user_promo_codes","users","vehicle_location_histories","vehicles"]} Error: json: cannot unmarshal object into Go value of type string ``` I have run the same query from the Swagger GUI and it parses the response properly: ``` { "tables": [ "NYCTaxiData", "NYCTaxiDataDim", "promo_codes", "rides", "rides_test", "user_promo_codes", "users", "vehicle_location_histories", "vehicles" ] } ``` ## Swagger specification [swagger.json.zip](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/files/11012159/swagger.json.zip) full source is available here: https://github.com/keith-mcclellan/startree-cli ## Steps to reproduce ## Environment swagger version: v0.30.4 go version go1.20.2 darwin/arm64 OS: MacOS

body:
## Problem statement

Getting the following error when directly or indirectly importing the `github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options` package:
```
classifier: unknown swagger annotation "extendee" 
```

## Swagger specification

2.0

## Steps to reproduce

Run the following command:
```
swagger generate spec -o swagger.json
```
On a go package with a single file like:
```
package swagtest

import (
	_ "github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options"
)
```

## Environment
swagger version: v0.30.4  
go version: go1.19.5 
OS: linux/amd

## Root Cause

Here's the problematic line in the `github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options` package: https://github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/blob/6adb6b499405b854abfe4ea19e60befc4231eb3e/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options/annotations.pb.go#L195

Here's the line:
```
	0,  // 0: grpc.gateway.protoc_gen_openapiv2.options.openapiv2_swagger:extendee -> google.protobuf.FileOptions 
```

The problem is that the regular expression for matching swagger annotations (`rxSwaggerAnnotation`) is matching the entire `"grpc.gateway.protoc_gen_openapiv2.options.openapiv2_swagger:extendee"` string above:

Here's the `rxSwaggerAnnotation` definition: https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/54644101dc9870a16814f8b18d0cb99e33619a17/codescan/regexprs.go#L31

While this could be seen as a problem on the `grpc-gateway` side, according to the goswagger annotation syntax documentation (https://goswagger.io/use/spec.html#annotation-syntax), the annotation prefix just `swagger:...` and in all documentation examples, the `swagger:` annotations are prefixed by a space. So it seems like a problem on the goswagger side. 

## Possible Fix
If I add a `\b`, the problem doesn't happen:
```
	rxSwaggerAnnotation  = regexp.MustCompile(`\bswagger:([\p{L}\p{N}\p{Pd}\p{Pc}]+)`)
```
`\b` is documented in https://pkg.go.dev/regexp/syntax as `at ASCII word boundary (\w on one side and \W, \A, or \z on the other)`, so when added to the beginning of `rxSwaggerAnnotation` it prevents it from matching the `"...openapiv2_swagger:extended"` string.

I don't know if adding `\b` would be the right solution (it doesn't work as Unicode boundary, for example; here's an article with an alternative albeit very incomplete option: https://shiba1014.medium.com/regex-word-boundaries-with-unicode-207794f6e7ed).

I also don't know what would be the broader implications of adding `\b` to the regexes, e.g. if it could fail existing 

## Workaround
Exclude the package:
```
swagger generate ... -x github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options
```

labels.name: generate spec
labels.description: Related to spec generation from code

title: classifier: unknown swagger annotation "extendee" when importing github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options

application/json // // Produces: // - application/json // // swagger:meta package main // swagger:route GET /enum/test GetEnumTest // responses: // 200: GetEnumTestResponse // Response of successful GetEnumTest // swagger:response GetEnumTestResponse type GetEnumTestResponseBody struct { // in: body body GetEnumTestResponse //nolint:unused } type GetEnumTestResponse struct { // The desctiption of the test enum in the response body TestEnumInBody TestEnum `json:"testEnumInBody"` } // swagger:parameters GetEnumTest type GetTestEnumParam struct { // The desctiption of the test enum param // in:query TestEnumInQueryParam TestEnum `json:"testEnumInQueryParam"` } // swagger:enum TestEnum type TestEnum string const ( TestEnumFirst TestEnum = "FIRST" TestEnumSecond TestEnum = "SECOND" TestEnumThird TestEnum = "THIRD" TestEnumFourth TestEnum = "FOURTH" ) ``` ## Swagger specification ```yaml basePath: / consumes: - application/json definitions: GetEnumTestResponse: properties: testEnumInBody: description: |- The desctiption of the test enum in the response body FIRST TestEnumFirst SECOND TestEnumSecond THIRD TestEnumThird FOURTH TestEnumFourth enum: - FIRST - SECOND - THIRD - FOURTH type: string x-go-enum-desc: |- FIRST TestEnumFirst SECOND TestEnumSecond THIRD TestEnumThird FOURTH TestEnumFourth x-go-name: TestEnumInBody type: object x-go-package: test_enum host: localhost:8080 info: description: Documentation of the Test Enum API. title: Test Enum version: 1.0.0 paths: /enum/test: get: operationId: GetEnumTest parameters: - description: |- The desctiption of the test enum param FIRST TestEnumFirst SECOND TestEnumSecond THIRD TestEnumThird FOURTH TestEnumFourth enum: - FIRST - SECOND - THIRD - FOURTH in: query name: testEnumInQueryParam type: string x-go-enum-desc: |- FIRST TestEnumFirst SECOND TestEnumSecond THIRD TestEnumThird FOURTH TestEnumFourth x-go-name: TestEnumInQueryParam responses: "200": $ref: '#/responses/GetEnumTestResponse' tags: - Trends produces: - application/json responses: GetEnumTestResponse: description: Response of successful GetEnumTest schema: $ref: '#/definitions/GetEnumTestResponse' schemes: - http swagger: "2.0" ``` ## Steps to reproduce ` swagger generate spec -o ./swagger.yaml` ## Environment swagger version: v0.30.4 go version: 1.20 OS: ``` uname -v Darwin Kernel Version 22.4.0: Mon Mar 6 21:00:17 PST 2023; root:xnu-8796.101.5~3/RELEASE_X86_64 ```

body:
## Problem statement

First off, thanks for `go-swagger`, it's a great tool and I'm sure it's not an easy one to maintain!

I recently tried upgrading from `go-swagger` v0.30.4 to v0.30.5 and noticed that some of the generated response structs are now empty.  I've tried to reduce it down to a minimal reproducible example below.

## Swagger specification

```
swagger: '2.0'
info:
  title: Users API
  version: 0.0.1

paths:
  /api/v1/users/:
    get:
      responses:
        200:
          $ref: '#/responses/Users'

responses:
  Users:
    description: List of users
    schema:
      allOf:
        - $ref: '#/definitions/APIResponse'
        - properties:
            data:
              $ref: '#/definitions/Users'

definitions:
  APIResponse:
    type: object
    properties:
      timestamp:
        type: string
  Users:
    type: array
    items:
      $ref: '#/definitions/User'
    x-omitempty: false
  User:
    type: object
    properties:
      id:
        type: integer
      admin:
        type: boolean
      name:
        type: string
```

## Steps to reproduce

Put the above spec in a file `swagger.yaml` and then run the following in the same directory as the `swagger.yaml` file using a v0.30.4 `go-swagger` binary:

```
rm -rf gen/client
mkdir -p gen/client
swagger-v0.30.4 generate client --target gen/client --name Users --spec swagger.yaml --name Users --client-package users --keep-spec-order
```

open the file `gen/client/users/operations/get_api_v1_users_responses.go` and note that the `GetAPIV1UsersOK` struct contains a `Payload` field:

```go
type GetAPIV1UsersOK struct {
        Payload *GetAPIV1UsersOKBody
}
```

Now, run the same command with a v0.30.5 `go-swagger` binary:

```
rm -rf gen/client
mkdir -p gen/client
swagger-v0.30.5 generate client --target gen/client --name Users --spec swagger.yaml --name Users --client-package users --keep-spec-order
```

and open the same `gen/client/users/operations/get_api_v1_users_responses.go` file and note that the `GetAPIV1UsersOK` struct is now empty:

```go
type GetAPIV1UsersOK struct {
}
```

Finally, run the command with a v0.30.5 `go-swagger` binary but omit the `--keep-spec-order` option:


```
rm -rf gen/client
mkdir -p gen/client
swagger-v0.30.5 generate client --target gen/client --name Users --spec swagger.yaml --name Users --client-package users
```

open the file `gen/client/users/operations/get_api_v1_users_responses.go` and note that the `GetAPIV1UsersOK` struct once again has a `Payload` field:

```go
type GetAPIV1UsersOK struct {
        Payload *GetAPIV1UsersOKBody
}
```

I am not by any means a Swagger expert but this behavior was very surprising to me.  Is the spec I'm using invalid or is this a regression of some sort on the part of `go-swagger`?  Please let me know if I can supply any more information that would be helpful.

## Environment
swagger version: 0.30.5
go version: 1.20
OS: CentOS 7

labels.name: swag
labels.description: Relates to github.com/go-openapi/swag

title: Use of --keep-spec-order leads to empty response structs in v0.30.5

body:
## Problem statement
When generating spec from source code, enum fields with a value type of `uint` are incorrectly parsed and have their resulting spec values defined as strings.
This is due to any `uint` type (`uint`, `uint8` , `uint16` , `uint32` , `uint64`) not being handled within [parseValueFromSchema](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/master/codescan/parser.go#L938).

## Steps to reproduce
Define a struct field as an enum with a value type of `uint`:
```
// enum:1,2,3
Example uint
```
Generate spec from the source code and look for the enum field and verify that it has its values defined like so:
```
"enum": [
  "1",
  "2",
  "3"
]
```

## Environment
go-swagger version: latest
go version: 1.20.4

It would be nice to have the documentation for a endpoint to show up in the definition hover in tools like vscode.

Example:
``` Go
type ClientService interface {

DeprovisionSystemDefinition deprovisions a system definition that was previously provisioned on the target cid
*/
func (a *Client) DeprovisionSystemDefinition(params *DeprovisionSystemDefinitionParams, opts ...ClientOption) (*DeprovisionSystemDefinitionOK, error) {

body:
Additional properties are lost when generating spec.

## Steps to reproduce

first generate a model using a spec that contains additional properties:

```yaml
swagger: "2.0"
info:
  version: "1.0.0"
  title: "Test"

paths: {}
definitions:
  test_additional_properties:
    type: object
    properties:
      field1:
        type: string
    additionalProperties:
      type: number
```
it results in the following model:

```go
// TestAdditionalProperties test additional properties
//
// swagger:model test_additional_properties
type TestAdditionalProperties struct {

	// field1
	Field1 string `json:"field1,omitempty"`

	// test additional properties
	TestAdditionalProperties map[string]float64 `json:"-"`
}
```

when this model is scanned back with generate spec, the additionalProperties are lost:

```yaml
definitions:
    test_additional_properties:
        description: TestAdditionalProperties test additional properties
        properties:
            field1:
                description: field1
                type: string
                x-go-name: Field1
        type: object
        x-go-name: TestAdditionalProperties
        x-go-package: github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/test_additional_prop/models
paths: {}
swagger: "2.0"
```

## Fix proposal

Annotate additional properties fileds with a special comment, for example like this (similar to examples in https://goswagger.io/use/spec/model.html):

```go
type TestAdditionalProperties struct {
	// test additional properties
        // Additional properties
	TestAdditionalProperties map[string]float64 `json:"-"`
}
```

Then the codescan will check for this comment and will create the correct spec.

## Environment
swagger version: v0.30.5-25-g10db6e00
go version: go1.18.3 linux/amd64
OS: Ubuntu 20.04

labels.description: Related to spec generation from code

body:
## Problem statement
The docs on [this page](https://goswagger.io/use/spec/response.html) provide an example of how to produce a swagger response spec from annotations. However running `swagger generate spec -o swagger.yaml` does not produce the example output.

## Swagger specification
I've copy pasted the example annotation in a test file in package main:
```go
package main

// A ValidationError is an error that is used when the required input fails validation.
// swagger:response validationError
type ValidationError struct {
	// The error message
	// in: body
	Body struct {
		// The validation message
		//
		// Required: true
		// Example: Expected type int
		Message string
		// An optional field name to which this validation applies
		FieldName string
	}
}
```

## Steps to reproduce
Output:
```yaml
paths: {}
responses:
    validationError:
        description: A ValidationError is an error that is used when the required input fails validation.
swagger: "2.0"
```

What it should be:
```yaml
---
responses:
  validationError:
    description: A ValidationError is an error that is used when the required input fails validation.
    schema:
      type: object
      description: The error message
      required:
      - Message
      properties:
        Message:
          type: string
          description: The validation message
          example: Expected type int
        FieldName:
          type: string
          description: an optional field name to which this validation applies
```

body:
## Problem statement
Currently, our API utilizes Bearer token authorization passed via the header, as defined in the OpenAPI specification:

```yaml
securityDefinitions:
  Bearer:
    type: apiKey
    name: Authorization
    in: header
```
However, we need to extend our authentication mechanism to support cookies session authentication as well.

## Swagger specification
### Context
OpenAPI 2.0 does not directly support cookies as an authentication mechanism, whereas OpenAPI 3.0 introduces support for cookies. We want to explore the possibility of integrating cookie-based authentication alongside our existing Bearer token authorization in our Go-swagger API.


### Questions
Is there a workaround within Go-swagger to implement cookie-based authentication alongside Bearer token authorization?

How can we ensure that the API seamlessly handles authentication based on whichever method (cookies or Bearer token) is provided by the client?

Are there any best practices or recommended approaches for integrating multiple authentication mechanisms in a Go-swagger API?

## Steps to reproduce

body:
## Problem statement

The generated server returns a validation error for a valid request.

Given the following spec:

- POST endpoint that accepts 2 possible encodings
- Accepts parameters inside form data
- One of the parameters (named `bar`) is of type `file` and  optional (required:false)

The generated server fails if I POST using `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` encoding, even if parameter `bar` (of type file, but optional) is not present. I think it’s fair enough for the autogenerated code to only accept `multipart/form-data` when `bar` is present in the request body, but if it’s not there, I would expect no validation at all for `bar` and no complains about request not being `multipart/form-data` since the content-type specified in the request is also accepted by the spec.

## Swagger specification

```yaml
swagger: '2.0'
info:
  version: '1.0'
  title: 'Test API'
paths:
  /test:
    post:
      consumes:
        - multipart/form-data
        - application/x-www-form-urlencoded
      parameters:
        - name: foo
          in: formData
          description: 'A string field'
          required: true
          type: string
        - name: bar
          in: formData
          description: 'A file field'
          required: false
          type: file
      responses:
        '201':
          description: 'Created'
        '500':
          description: 'Internal Server Error'
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Build server from spec: `./go-swagger generate server -f spec.yaml`
2. Run server: `go run cmd/test-api-server/main.go`
3. On another terminal send a request with curl using `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` encoding and without the `bar` field:

```bash
curl -H 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' -X POST http://127.0.0.1:56774/test -d "foo=test"

{"code":400,"message":"reading file \"bar\" failed: request Content-Type isn't multipart/form-data"}
```


## Environment
swagger version: v0.31.0
go version: 1.22.2
OS:  mac OS & Linux

labels.description: Relates to github.com/go-openapi/runtime

body:
## Problem statement

When generating models the response payload types for each endpoint are created as unique types, even if they are referencing the same type in the spec.

These unique types are not "related" in any way in go. This makes it hard to work with in client code because, other than using reflection, there is no generic way to deal with common classes of errors. Go generics provide no way to generically write a handler that works for structs with common fields. Additionally, there are no accessors for the fields so interfaces can't be used. If your API has 200 endpoints and every single one can return a "Forbidden" error it means you need 200 different bits of code to check the error type, do the cast, and handle the error's properties.

*Why not re-use `Payload` types globally that are defined in the `#/responses/...` list in the spec?*

## Swagger specification

Multiple endpoints all share many of the same response types, especially for common error categories:

```
paths:
    "/foo":
        get:
            operationId: GetFoo
            responses:
                "200":
                    $ref: "#/responses/UniqueResponseTypeForThisEndpoint"
                "401":
                    $ref: "#/responses/NotAuthorized"
                "403":
                    $ref: "#/responses/Forbidden"
                "422":
                    $ref: "#/responses/ValidationError"
                "500":
                    $ref: "#/responses/InternalErrorResponse"
    "/bar":
        get:
            operationId: GetBar
            responses:
                "200":
                    $ref: "#/responses/OtherResponseTypeForThatEndpoint"
                "401":
                    $ref: "#/responses/NotAuthorized"
                "403":
                    $ref: "#/responses/Forbidden"
                "422":
                    $ref: "#/responses/ValidationError"
                "500":
                    $ref: "#/responses/InternalErrorResponse"
```

This results in a different implementation of response types with unique (but identical) Payload types. For example
* `type GetBarForbidden struct`
* `type GetFooForbidden struct`

## Steps to reproduce

Have two endpoints use the same response models and generate a client.

## Environment
swagger version: 0.31.0 
go version: 1.18.0  
OS:   

Thanks for the tool!
title: Generated response types are duplicated, making them hard to work with

func TestCmd_Mixin_NoError(t *testing.T) {

body:
## Problem statement
Currently, `go-swagger` is only considering comments as part of `GenDecl` nodes, and not of the `TypeSpec` nodes themselves. This means that for code like
```go
type (
	// description/title of enum
	// swagger:enum EnumType
	EnumType string
)
```
comments are going to be ignored. Comment parsing should consider both sources: `GenDecl` and `TypeSpec` nodes.

## Environment
swagger version: `master` (`5226f2c6fcc7705caaab26862c941370699dbd97`)
go version: `1.23.10`
OS: `windows/amd64`

body:
## Problem statement
Currently, only basic information like title and description is being parsed and used for typed primitives referenced by top-level models.

Example: Let's say there's a model `Order` containing a `State` enum:
```go
type Order struct {
    State State `json:"state"`
}

// State represents the state of an order.
// enum: ["created","processed"]
type State string
```
Surprisingly, the enum declaration of `State` will be used as its description in this case.

body:
Linting is certainly not our first priority, but we definitely need to keep our large code base in good shape and with a reasonable maintainability status.

A first shot has been fired by @thaJeztah (#3233,  #3234).
Another more general pass is proposed by #3236, but still partial.

What is needed is:

1. Explain clearly why some linters are disabled (#3236 started to comment .golangci.yml configuration - this should be documented in the CONTRIBUTING notes)
2. Disable linters more sparingly: on many an occasion, we disable a useful check when only a few code sections really need it to be disabled with a` `//nolint  directive
3. Be explicit why a  //nolint   directive has been added, with an additional comment on the same line. Need to audit all such existing directives and figure out why we have added those
  * [x] done (cmd)
  * [x] done (generator)
  * [ ] todo (codescan) 
4. Using linters auto-fix while preparing #3236 introduced a few unexpected (an unwanted) changes that had to be manually reverted. Action: find out which linter is the culprit and report it as a bug to the linter's auto
5. The hard part, most likely split in several PRs
   * [x] relint _everything_ on master (generator): #3259 
   * [x] ... (cmd) : #3240  
   * [ ] ... (codescan): #3261  (partial) => #3307  moved the problem to https://github.com/go-openapi/codescan.

A few other remarks:

* many (perhaps most) issues pertain to the "diff" feature
   * what I've always wished for that feature is to move it away as a go-openapi lib (like in go-openapi/analysis) where it would be a better fit,
      could be used standalone - I don't want to maintain rich features under the cmd/swagger package any longer.
  * Action plan: relint, move (note to self: make sure past contributions and original commit signaturs are kept when swapping repo)

* the codescan package is about to be subject to a massive refactoring (there are a few pending PRs to be merged before that) - because it has become just impossible to maintain, and that's not a matter of linting, but structure -
  * do not waste too much time on relinting codescan

Refactoring tests (perhaps deserves an open issue of its own)
*  I've have forked stretrch/testify on go-openapi to make it dependency-free and merge a few desirable features there => adopt it
* continue the long-standing obscure work of refactoring tests so they look more readable and easy to learn. Currently identified as a major pain to contribute to go-swagger


  **UPDATE**: #3307 and #3308  have moved codescan and diff to different repos where they are taken care of more cautiously (no longer drowned in the mass of code).
title: code renovation: need to modernize our approach to linting

body:
I was experimenting with the swagger output generated using `go-swagger` in a repository, and I combined it with [swagger-typescript-api](https://github.com/acacode/swagger-typescript-api) to generate typescript types, however I noticed that everything is optional by default. Making the generated types pretty unusable.

However, the `go-swagger generate spec` is nicely adding the `x-nullable` to all properties that may be null; moreover the go struct use nicely the json synthax ``json:"<name>,omitempty"`` to specify if something may be missing. Isn't it enough to specify in the other cases that something is required?

I understand that we can add the `// required:true` annotation, but in a codebase with hundreds of struct, this may takes some time, so asking for guidance. Thanks !

labels.name: generate spec
labels.description: Related to spec generation from code

title: question(generate spec) : generating spec from go types, specify required without explicit comment
